title: Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW)
text: Founded in 2022, the Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW) brings together political scientists from the University of Osnabrück, the University of Duisburg-Essen, and Witten/Herdecke University to discuss ongoing research and new ideas. It aims to give regular feedback to its participants, in particular to emerging scholars at the PhD and post-doctoral levels in the fields of conflict research and contentious politics. Online workshops take place monthly. Once a year, we organize a joint retreat.

title: Conflict Research Exchange Workshop (CREW)
text:
Members




Alexander De Juan


Professor of Comparative Politics, University of Osnabrück


Alexander De Juan’s research focuses on political attitudes and behavior in the context of conflict and autocratic rule with a particular interest in historical episodes of contention.





Johannes Vüllers


Professor of International Relations, University of Duisburg-Essen


Johannes Vüllers studies contentious politics, civil wars, and conflict resolution with a regional expertise on Southeast Asia.





Nils-Christian Bormann


Professor of International Political Studies, Witten/Herdecke University


Nils-Christian Bormann investigates the causes and consequences of political violence, power-sharing coalitions, and group-based inequality.





Teresa Hummler


Post-Doc, University of Duisburg Essen


Teresa Hummler is studying the influence of contentious politics on organizational development and individual political attitudes. In her PhD thesis, she investigated how neighborhood characteristics shape individuals’ political support.

title: Data
text:
Citizen Anger Interwar News (CAIN) Data


The Citizen Anger Interwar News (CAIN) Data covers political violence events reports from ten European interwar democracies. It records their location, timing, the number of casualties, and actors involved. The interactive map allows you to explore the data.


Dataset Interactive Map







Democratic Electoral Systems


Information on electoral rules in 1,826 legislative elections and 648 presidential elections in democracies since 1919. The dataset spans two time periods covered in separate data publications: the interwar period (1919–1945) and the postwar period (1946–2020).


Interwar Data (1919–1945) Postwar Data (1946–2020)







Archive of Interwar Europe Election Data & Assemblies (AIEEDA)


A multi-level dataset of parliamentary elections in interwar Europe (1919–1939), covering 137 elections across 25 democracies. Includes electoral results, ideological and organizational data for 401 parties and 35 alliances, and cabinet data.


Dataset Paper







Ethnic Power Relations (EPR)


Data on ethnic groups’ access to state power, their settlement patterns, links to rebel organizations, and transborder ethnic kin. The EPR dataset family is maintained by the International Conflict Research group at ETH Zürich.


Dataset







Linking Ethnic Data from Africa (LEDA)


A systematic approach to linking over 8,100 ethnic categories from 11 databases on ethnic groups in Africa. Includes the LEDA open-source software package to help researchers merge ethnic data across datasets.


Paper PDF

title: Teaching
text:
Below you find some of the classes I teach regularly. I am open to supervising BA, MA, or PhD theses with an empirical focus. If you are interested in working with me, prepare a short overview of the research question you are interested in, and how you would like to investigate it.
If you would like to discuss teaching or study-related issues please sign up for office hours here.

title: Nils-Christian Bormann
text:
Department of Philosophy, Politics, and Economics · Witten/Herdecke University

I am Professor of International Political Studies in the Department of Philosophy, Politics, and Economics at Witten/Herdecke University and an affiliate at the Politics of Inequality Cluster at the University of Konstanz. I study political violence, ethnic politics, power-sharing coalitions, and democratic survival. My research has been published in the American Journal of Political Science, the American Political Science Review, the Journal of Politics, and has been supported by the European Research Council.
CV
